column6: and switches.

Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: recall and apply $V = I R$; understand how resistance affects current.

Description: The total energy transferred by an appliance depends on its power rating and the duration for which it is used.

Description: A charged object creates an electric field that exerts a force on other charges

Name: Density of materials
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: recall and apply $\rho = m / V$; explain density differences between states using the particle model.
Description: Density measures how much mass is concentrated in a given volume and is determined by the arrangement and spacing of atoms or molecules.

Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: apply $\Delta E = m c \Delta\theta$; define specific heat capacity.

Name: Pressure in gases (physics only)

Name: Increasing the pressure of a gas (HT only)
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: explain how doing work on a gas increases its internal energy and temperature.

column5: such as using a bicycle pump

column7: increasing their internal energy and raising the temperature.

Name: The structure of an atom

Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: describe the transition from plum pudding to nuclear models; explain the contributions of Bohr and Chadwick.

Name: Radioactive decay and nuclear radiation
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: define activity and count-rate; compare alpha
Description: beta
column5: and gamma radiation; evaluate sources for specific uses.
column6: Unstable nuclei undergo random radioactive decay by emitting alpha particles
column10: each with distinct penetrating and ionising powers.

Name: Nuclear equations
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: write and balance nuclear equations for alpha and beta decay.
Description: Nuclear equations represent decay by showing changes in mass and atomic numbers
column5: where alpha decay reduces both and beta decay increases the atomic number without changing mass.

Name: Half-lives and the random nature of radioactive decay
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: define half-life; determine half-life from graphs; (HT only) calculate net decline ratios.

Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: distinguish between contamination and irradiation; compare associated hazards.
Description: Contamination is the physical presence of radioactive material on an object
column5: whereas irradiation is simply exposure to radiation without the object becoming radioactive itself.

Name: Background radiation
Learning Outcomes: Students should be able to: identify natural and man-made sources of radiation; understand factors affecting radiation dose.
Description: Background radiation is constant and originates from rocks
column5: cosmic rays
column6: and human activities like nuclear testing
